title: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy
authors: JoAnn E. Manson, Shari S. Bassuk

items.subtopic: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding FSH Dynamics in Perimenopause vs. Menopause
items.content: During perimenopause, follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) levels rise due to altered folliculogenesis and reduced inhibin secretion, leading to 'irregularly irregular' hormone levels. In contrast, menopause is marked by consistently high FSH and low estradiol levels.
keyPoints: Perimenopause: Characterized by irregular FSH and estradiol levels due to altered folliculogenesis and reduced inhibin secretion.
Menopause: Consistently high FSH (>25 IU/L in a random sample) and low estradiol levels, reflecting loss of inhibin and estrogen feedback.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS

items.subtopic: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS
items.title: Using FSH Levels to Assess Menopausal Transition and Fertility
items.content: FSH levels are used to diagnose perimenopause and assess fertility. A consistently low FSH level in the early follicular phase does not support a diagnosis of perimenopause, while levels >25 IU/L indicate late menopause transition. Day 3 FSH levels can predict pregnancy likelihood: <20 IU/L (good), 20-<30 IU/L (fair), ≥30 IU/L (poor).
keyPoints: FSH level >25 IU/L in a random sample characterizes the late menopausal transition.
Day 3 FSH levels: <20 IU/L indicates good fertility, 20 to <30 IU/L fair, and ≥30 IU/L poor likelihood of pregnancy.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS

items.title: Identifying Menopausal Transition Symptoms
items.content: Menopausal transition symptoms such as hot flashes, night sweats, irregular bleeding, and vaginal dryness are strongly linked to ovarian senescence. However, mood swings, depression, impaired memory or concentration, somatic symptoms, urinary incontinence, and sexual dysfunction have inconclusive evidence linking them directly to menopause.
keyPoints: Strong evidence links hot flashes, night sweats, irregular bleeding, and vaginal dryness to ovarian senescence.
Moderate evidence supports sleep disturbances as a symptom of the menopausal transition.
Inconclusive evidence for mood swings, depression, impaired memory or concentration, somatic symptoms, urinary incontinence, and sexual dysfunction being majorly caused by ovarian aging.
Nearly 60% of women reported hot flashes within 2 years before their final menses.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · SYMPTOMS | PERIMENOPAUSAL THERAPY

items.title: Management of Perimenopausal Symptoms with Hormonal Therapy
items.content: Low-dose combined oral contraceptives are effective for managing irregular or heavy menses and hormone-related symptoms that impair quality of life during perimenopause. They eliminate vasomotor symptoms, restore regular cyclicity, and offer additional benefits such as protection against ovarian and endometrial cancers and increased bone density.
keyPoints: Low-dose combined oral contraceptives (e.g., 20 µg ethinyl estradiol and 1 mg norethindrone acetate daily for 21 days each month) are used to manage perimenopausal symptoms.
Benefits include elimination of vasomotor symptoms, restoration of regular cyclicity, protection against ovarian and endometrial cancers, and increased bone density.
Contraindications: cigarette smoking, liver disease, history of thromboembolism or cardiovascular disease, breast cancer, unexplained vaginal bleeding.
Progestin-only formulations (e.g., 0.35 mg norethindrone daily) or medroxyprogesterone injections (150 mg IM every 3 months) are alternatives for women with contraindications to estrogen.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · SYMPTOMS | PERIMENOPAUSAL THERAPY

items.subtopic: TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT
items.title: Guidelines for Transitioning from Contraceptive Hormones to Menopausal Hormone Therapy (HT)
items.content: For sexually active women using contraceptive hormones to alleviate perimenopausal symptoms, the decision to switch to menopausal hormone therapy must be individualized. Women should consider switching to a barrier method of contraception if they are willing, as doses in HT are lower than those in oral contraceptives and do not prevent pregnancy. The average age of final menses among relatives can guide when to initiate this process.
keyPoints: HT doses are lower than those in oral contraceptives and do not prevent pregnancy.
A 1-year absence of spontaneous menses indicates ovulation cessation.
Women should switch to a barrier method if willing, or resume oral contraceptives if menses occur spontaneously.
Average age of final menses among relatives can guide the initiation process.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT

items.subtopic: TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT
items.title: Decision-Making for Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy (HT)
items.content: The decision to use postmenopausal HT is complex, balancing benefits and risks. While observational studies suggest HT prevents cardiovascular diseases, randomized trials like the Women's Health Initiative have not confirmed these benefits. The WHI study found an unfavorable benefit-risk ratio in the estrogen-progestin arm and excess stroke risk without reduced CHD risk in the estrogen-only arm.
keyPoints: Nearly 40% of postmenopausal women aged 50–74 used HT in 2000 despite limited data on health consequences.
Observational studies suggest benefits, but randomized trials like WHI have not confirmed them.
WHI study involved >27,000 women aged 50–79 (mean age, 63) for 5–7 years.
WHI found unfavorable benefit-risk ratio in estrogen-progestin arm and excess stroke risk without CHD reduction in estrogen-only arm.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT

items.subtopic: BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.
items.title: Effectiveness of Estrogen Therapy for Menopausal Symptoms
items.content: Estrogen therapy is highly effective in controlling vasomotor and genitourinary symptoms associated with menopause, supported by compelling evidence from randomized clinical trials. Nonhormonal alternatives like paroxetine mesylate, fezolinetant, gabapentin, oxybutynin, and others are less effective.
keyPoints: Estrogen therapy is highly effective for vasomotor and genitourinary symptoms.
Nonhormonal drugs such as paroxetine mesylate (7.5 mg/d) and fezolinetant (45 mg/d) are approved by the FDA for treating vasomotor symptoms but are less effective than hormone therapy.
HT reduces risk for menopausal symptoms by 65–90%.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.

items.subtopic: BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.
items.title: WHI Study on Hormone Therapy and Cardiovascular Outcomes
items.content: The Women's Health Initiative (WHI) study assessed the effects of hormone therapy, specifically conjugated equine estrogens with or without medroxyprogesterone acetate, over several years. It found a significant interaction by age in cardiovascular outcomes, indicating different risks for younger versus older women.
keyPoints: The WHI estrogen-progestin arm assessed 5.6 years of conjugated equine estrogens (0.625 mg/d) plus medroxyprogesterone acetate (2.5 mg/d).
The WHI estrogen-alone arm assessed 7.1 years of conjugated equine estrogens (0.625 mg/d).
There was a significant interaction by age in the association between HT and cardiovascular outcomes.
Risk reduction observed during a cumulative 13-year follow-up period.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.

items.title: Impact of Estrogen Therapy on Bone Mineral Density
items.content: Estrogen therapy significantly increases bone mineral density in postmenopausal women, reducing the risk of fractures.
keyPoints: Postmenopausal estrogen therapy increases bone mineral density by 4–6% at the spine and 2–3% at the hip.
These increases are maintained during treatment.
Estrogen therapy reduces vertebral fracture risk by 50–80% and hip, wrist, and other peripheral fractures by 25–30%.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 1)

items.title: Role of Bisphosphonates and Other Therapies in Osteoporosis
items.content: Bisphosphonates, denosumab, bazedoxifene, raloxifene, and teriparatide are effective in increasing bone mass density and reducing fracture rates.
keyPoints: Bisphosphonates (e.g., alendronate, risedronate) reduce bone resorption and decrease fracture rates.
Denosumab (60 mg twice per year SC) increases bone mass density.
Raloxifene (60 mg/d) and teriparatide (20 µg/d SC) are alternative therapies without adverse effects on the endometrium or breast.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 1)

items.title: Risks Associated with Estrogen Therapy
items.content: Estrogen therapy, particularly unopposed estrogen, is associated with increased risks of endometrial cancer and venous thromboembolism.
keyPoints: Unopposed estrogen use for 1–5 years triples the risk of endometrial cancer; long-term use increases it nearly tenfold.
Progestogen use eliminates or reduces this risk.
Oral estrogen use is associated with a 2.5-fold increase in venous thromboembolism risk, and transdermal estrogen may be safer.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 1)

items.title: Increased Breast Cancer Risk with Estrogen-Progestin Therapy
items.content: Estrogen-progestin therapy is associated with a higher risk of breast cancer compared to estrogen alone, particularly as the duration of use increases.
keyPoints: Relative risks (RRs) for incident breast cancer increase significantly with longer durations of combined hormone therapy: 1.60 (95% CI, 1.52–1.69) for 1-4 years, up to 2.51 (95% CI, 2.35–2.68) for ≥15 years.
Estrogen alone shows lower RRs: 1.17 (1.10–1.26) for 1-4 years and 1.58 (1.51–1.66) for ≥15 years.
WHI trial indicates a 24% increased risk of breast cancer with combination hormones over an average of 5.6 years, while estrogen-only therapy showed no increase in risk.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 2)

items.title: Gallbladder Disease Risk with Hormone Therapy
items.content: Postmenopausal women taking oral estrogen have a significantly increased risk of developing gallstones or requiring cholecystectomy.
keyPoints: WHI trial reports ~55% higher likelihood of gallbladder disease in women on estrogen-progestin or estrogen alone compared to placebo.
Observational studies suggest a two- to threefold increase in risk for those taking oral estrogen.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 2)

items.title: Lack of Cardiovascular Benefit from Hormone Therapy
items.content: Randomized trials do not support the cardioprotective effects of hormone therapy in postmenopausal women, particularly those with preexisting cardiovascular disease.
keyPoints: Observational studies suggested a 35–50% reduction in CHD incidence with estrogen use, but randomized trials like HERS and ESPRIT found no significant benefit.
HERS trial showed a 50% increase in coronary events risk during the first year of active treatment.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 2)

items.title: Timing of Hormone Therapy Initiation and CHD Risk
items.content: The timing of hormone therapy initiation post-menopause significantly influences the risk of coronary heart disease (CHD). Early initiation may confer cardiovascular benefits by slowing atherosclerosis, while late initiation can increase risks due to prothrombotic effects in women with subclinical lesions.
keyPoints: WHI trial showed 18% increased CHD risk with estrogen-progestin therapy over placebo, though not statistically significant overall.
First-year of WHI trial had an 80% increased CHD risk (p for trend by time = .03).
Estrogen may slow early atherosclerosis but worsen advanced lesions.
Timing hypothesis: Early HT initiation in women with less arterial damage may be beneficial, while late initiation can increase risks.
Nonhuman primate studies showed 70% plaque reduction when hormones were given immediately after oophorectomy.
ELITE trial (p for interaction = .007) found slowed carotid atherosclerosis progression with estrogen in women within 6 years of menopause onset, not beyond 10 years post-menopause.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 3)

items.title: Impact of Hormone Therapy on Atherosclerosis Progression
items.content: The effect of hormone therapy on atherosclerosis progression is dependent on the timing relative to menopause onset, with early initiation showing potential benefits in slowing progression.
keyPoints: ELITE trial demonstrated significant slowing of carotid atherosclerotic progression with estrogen within 6 years of menopause (mean age, 55.4 years).
No effect on carotid atherosclerosis progression was observed in women >10 years past menopause onset (mean age, 65.4 years) in ELITE trial.
KEEPS trial found no impact on carotid atherosclerotic progression with either oral conjugated estrogens or transdermal estradiol in women within 3 years of menopause onset (mean age, 53 years).
Low prevalence of atherosclerosis endpoints in KEEPS may have limited the study's power to detect differences.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 3)

items.title: Timing Hypothesis in Hormone Therapy and CHD Risk
items.content: The timing of hormone therapy initiation relative to menopause significantly influences cardiovascular outcomes, particularly coronary heart disease (CHD). Women who begin hormone therapy within 4 years of menopause may experience a lower risk of CHD compared to those starting ≥10 years after menopause.
keyPoints: Women with favorable cholesterol profiles had a 40% lower risk of incident CHD with estrogen ± progestin.
Worse cholesterol profile led to a 73% higher risk (p for interaction = .02).
Metabolic syndrome presence doubled CHD risk with hormone therapy, no association without it.
Estrogen-only therapy showed a 40% CHD risk reduction in women aged 50–59, 5% reduction at 60–69, and 9% increase at 70–79 (p for trend by age = .08).
Total myocardial infarction: estrogen alone had a 45% reduction in youngest group, 24% increase in oldest (p for trend by age = .02).
Estrogen-progestin therapy reduced CHD risk by 9% in women <10 years past menopause, increased by 16% at 10–19 years, and doubled at >20 years past menopause (p for trend = .01).
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 4)

items.title: Hormone Therapy and Stroke Risk
items.content: Both estrogen-progestin and estrogen-only therapies are associated with an increased risk of stroke, approximately 35% higher than placebo in WHI participants. The influence of age at initiation on this risk remains unclear.
keyPoints: Estrogen-progestin or estrogen alone increases stroke risk by ~35% compared to placebo.
HT is linked to excess stroke risk across all age groups in both WHI and Nurses' Health Study.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 4)

items.title: Hormone Therapy's Impact on Colorectal Cancer Risk
items.content: Hormone therapy, particularly estrogen-progestin, is associated with a significant reduction in colorectal cancer risk, though this benefit varies by age.
keyPoints: Observational studies suggest HT reduces colon and rectal cancer risks (8-34% relative benefits).
WHI showed a 38% reduction in colorectal cancer with estrogen-progestin over 5.6 years.
No benefit observed with 7 years of estrogen-only therapy.
Age modifying effect: doubling of risk at HT in women aged 70–79 (p for trend by age = .02).
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 4)

items.title: HT and Ovarian Cancer Risk
items.content: Hormone therapy (HT), particularly estrogen-progestin, is associated with an increased risk of ovarian cancer.
keyPoints: WHI data supports the hypothesis that HT increases ovarian cancer risk.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 5)

items.title: HT and Endometrial Cancer Risk
items.content: Estrogen-progestin use in the WHI showed a nonsignificant reduction in endometrial cancer risk during active treatment, with significant reduction emerging post-treatment.
keyPoints: Nonsignificant 17% reduction in endometrial cancer risk during HT.
Significant reduction in risk emerged during postintervention period.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 5)

items.title: Long-term Effects of Estrogen-Progestin on Breast Cancer
items.content: Post-discontinuation of estrogen-progestin therapy, there is a persistent elevated risk of breast cancer.
keyPoints: Elevated breast cancer risk persisted with RR = 1.28 during median cumulative 13-year follow-up.
Significant elevation in breast cancer mortality (RR = 1.35; p = .11) observed over 20 years.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 5)

items.title: Indications and Contraindications for Systemic Hormone Therapy
items.content: Systemic hormone therapy is primarily indicated for moderate to severe vasomotor symptoms unresponsive to lifestyle modifications. It may also be used for osteoporosis prevention in high-risk women intolerant of other treatments. Absolute risks increase with age, necessitating a thorough review of benefits and risks with patients.
keyPoints: Primary indication: Moderate to severe hot flashes/night sweats not alleviated by lifestyle changes.
Secondary use: Osteoporosis prevention in high-risk women intolerant to other therapies.
Absolute risk increases with age, influencing decision-making.

Contraindications include unexplained vaginal bleeding, liver disease, venous thromboembolism, untreated hypertension, history of certain cancers (e.g., endometrial cancer except stage 1 without deep invasion), and cardiovascular events.
Relative contraindications: Elevated risk of breast cancer, hypertriglyceridemia (>400 mg/dL), elevated cardiovascular disease risk, active gallbladder disease.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Postmenopausal HT (part 1) | Postmenopausal HT (part 2)

items.title: Therapeutic Options and Duration for Systemic Hormone Therapy
items.content: Systemic hormone therapy options vary, with estrogen alone recommended for women without a uterus and combined estrogen-progestogen for those with a uterus. Short-term use is advised for symptom relief in women without contraindications, while long-term use requires careful consideration of breast cancer risk.
keyPoints: Estrogen alone: Recommended for women post-hysterectomy.
Estrogen plus progestogen: For women with an intact uterus.
Common oral estrogens: 17β-estradiol (1.0 or 0.5 mg/d) and conjugated equine estrogens (CEE; 0.625, 0.45, or 0.3 mg/d).
Transdermal estrogen: 17β-estradiol skin patches (0.035 or 0.05 mg/d).
Common progestogens: Medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA; 2.5, 5, or 10 mg/d) and micronized progesterone (100 or 200 mg/d).
Short-term use (<5 years for estrogen-progestogen, <7 years for estrogen alone) recommended for symptom relief without contraindications.
Long-term use requires careful consideration of breast cancer risk.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Postmenopausal HT (part 1) | Postmenopausal HT (part 2)

items.title: Alternative Treatments and Monitoring for Women with Contraindications
items.content: Women contraindicated or opposed to hormone therapy may benefit from certain antidepressants, fezolinetant, gabapentin, or oxybutynin. Regular reassessment of benefits and risks is crucial, especially if health status changes.
keyPoints: Alternatives for those contraindicated/opposed to HT: Antidepressants (paroxetine, venlafaxine), fezolinetant, gabapentin, oxybutynin.
Reassessment of benefits and risks recommended every 6–12 months or if health status changes.
Immediate discontinuation of HT advised for women experiencing acute coronary events or stroke.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Postmenopausal HT (part 1) | Postmenopausal HT (part 2)

items.subtopic: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS
items.question: Which hormone level is consistently high during menopause?
items.explanation: FSH levels are consistently high in menopause due to reduced inhibin and estrogen feedback.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS

items.subtopic: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS
items.question: What pattern of hormone levels is observed with the transition into menopause?
options: Estradiol levels rise, estrone levels fall
Both estradiol and estrone levels fall
Estradiol levels fall, estrone levels are preserved
items.explanation: With menopause transition, estradiol levels fall markedly while estrone levels are relatively preserved.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS

items.subtopic: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS
items.question: Which phase of the menstrual cycle is used to assess fertility based on FSH levels?
items.explanation: FSH levels measured on day 3 of the early follicular phase can indicate fertility likelihood.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS

items.question: Which symptom is strongly evidenced to be caused by the menopausal transition?
items.explanation: The passage states there is strong evidence that the menopausal transition can cause hot flashes.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · SYMPTOMS | PERIMENOPAUSAL THERAPY

items.question: What percentage of women reported hot flashes in a U.S. study during the 2 years before their final menses?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that nearly 60% of women reported hot flashes in the specified period.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · SYMPTOMS | PERIMENOPAUSAL THERAPY

items.question: Which symptom is not conclusively linked to ovarian aging according to the passage?
items.explanation: The passage states that there is inconclusive or insufficient evidence linking ovarian aging to mood swings.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · SYMPTOMS | PERIMENOPAUSAL THERAPY

items.subtopic: TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT
items.question: When considering switching from contraceptive hormones to hormone therapy (HT) for perimenopausal symptoms, what factor can serve as a guide for when to initiate the process?
options: The average age of final menses among relatives.
The patient's preference for barrier methods.
The duration of contraceptive hormone use.
items.explanation: The average age of final menses among relatives can guide when to initiate the process of switching from contraceptive hormones to HT.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT

items.subtopic: TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT
items.question: What is a reliable indicator that ovulation has ceased in women using oral contraceptives?
items.explanation: A 1-year absence of spontaneous menses reliably indicates ovulation cessation, although it cannot be assessed while on oral contraceptives.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT

items.subtopic: TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT
items.question: What is a key reason for the challenge in balancing the benefits and risks of postmenopausal hormone therapy (HT) for individual patients?
options: The lack of any observational studies.
The conclusive data on health consequences.
The paucity of conclusive data until recently.
The uniform effectiveness across all women.
items.explanation: Balancing the benefits and risks is challenging due to the paucity of conclusive data on the health consequences of postmenopausal HT until recently.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT

items.subtopic: BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.
items.question: Which nonhormonal drug is approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration for treatment of vasomotor symptoms?
items.explanation: Paroxetine mesylate is the only nonhormonal drug approved by the FDA for treatment of vasomotor symptoms.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.

items.subtopic: BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.
items.question: What was the duration of hormone therapy in the estrogen-progestin arm of the WHI?
items.explanation: The estrogen-progestin arm of the WHI assessed 5.6 years of hormone therapy.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.

items.subtopic: BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.
items.question: Which combination has been approved for treatment of vasomotor symptoms?
items.explanation: Bazedoxifene in combination with conjugated estrogens has been approved for treatment of vasomotor symptoms.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.

items.question: Which therapy is associated with a rapid increase in bone mineral density at the spine and hip among postmenopausal women?
items.explanation: Estrogen therapy rapidly increases bone mineral density at the spine and hip.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 1)

items.question: What is the effect of adding a progestogen to estrogen therapy on fracture risk reduction?
options: It does not modify the benefit
items.explanation: Addition of a progestogen does not appear to modify the fracture risk reduction benefit.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 1)

items.question: Which alternative therapy to estrogen does NOT have adverse effects on the endometrium or breast?
items.explanation: Both bisphosphonates and raloxifene do not have adverse effects on the endometrium or breast, unlike estrogen.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 1)

items.question: What is a recommended daily intake of calcium to reduce osteoporosis-related fractures?
items.explanation: Adequate calcium intake is recommended at 1000–1200 mg/d.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 1)

items.question: Which therapy has been shown to increase breast cancer risk more than estrogen alone?
items.explanation: Combined estrogen-progestin regimens increase breast cancer risk more than estrogen alone.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 2)

items.question: According to the WHI, what was observed about the incidence of breast cancer with estrogen-progestin therapy compared to placebo?
options: 24% more likely to develop breast cancer
7.1 years of estrogen-only therapy increased risk
items.explanation: Women assigned to receive combination hormones were 24% more likely to develop breast cancer than those on placebo.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 2)

items.question: What was the relative risk for incident breast cancer with current use of estrogen-progestin for ≥15 years compared to never use?
items.explanation: The relative risk was 2.51 for current use of estrogen-progestin for ≥15 years.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 2)

items.question: What is a potential reason why the association between HT and increased breast cancer risk may be inconclusive?
options: Higher rates of screening mammography in HT users
Lower incidence of breast cancer in non-HT users
Increased use of progestin mitigating estrogen's effects
items.explanation: The association may be inconclusive due to higher rates of screening mammography and earlier detection in HT users.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 2)

items.question: What was observed regarding the effect of estrogen-progestin therapy on CHD risk in the WHI trial?
options: There was a significant long-term increase in CHD risk.
Women were 18% more likely to develop CHD, but this was not statistically significant.
Estrogen-progestin therapy significantly reduced CHD risk throughout the study.
No difference in CHD risk was observed compared to placebo.
items.explanation: Women assigned to estrogen-progestin therapy were 18% more likely to develop CHD, but this increase was not statistically significant over the entire duration of the trial.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 3)

items.question: What did data from nonhuman primate studies suggest about estrogen administration and atherosclerosis?
options: Estrogen increased the extent of coronary artery plaque in early stages.
Estrogen had no effect on coronary artery plaque regardless of timing.
Immediate post-oophorectomy administration reduced plaque by 70%.
Delayed estrogen administration significantly reduced plaque.
items.explanation: Administration of exogenous hormones immediately after oophorectomy, during the early stages of atherosclerosis, reduced the extent of plaque by 70%.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 3)

items.question: According to the ELITE trial, what was the effect of estrogen on carotid atherosclerotic progression?
options: Estrogen significantly slowed progression in women more than 10 years past menopause.
Estrogen had no effect on carotid atherosclerosis at any stage.
Estrogen significantly slowed progression within 6 years of menopause onset.
items.explanation: Oral $17\beta$-estradiol significantly slowed carotid atherosclerotic progression in women within 6 years of menopause onset but not in those more than 10 years past menopause.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 3)

items.question: In the WHI trial, what was the observed effect of estrogen-progestin therapy on CHD risk among women aged 70–79?
options: No significant change in CHD risk
A twofold increase in CHD risk
items.explanation: Estrogen-progestin therapy was associated with a twofold increase in CHD risk among women aged 70–79.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 4)

items.question: Which age group experienced the greatest reduction in CHD risk with estrogen-only therapy in the WHI trial?
items.explanation: Estrogen-only therapy was associated with a 40% reduction in CHD risk among women aged 50–59.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 4)

items.question: What trend did the WHI trial find regarding total myocardial infarction and estrogen-progestin therapy based on years since menopause?
options: A consistent risk reduction across all time periods
No significant change in risk with years since menopause
An increased risk with more years past menopause
A decreased risk with more years past menopause
items.explanation: CHD risks increased with years since menopause, with a significantly elevated risk among women who were ≥20 years past menopause.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 4)

items.question: According to the WHI trial, how did estrogen-progestin therapy affect colorectal cancer risk in women aged 70–79?
items.explanation: HT was associated with a doubling of colorectal cancer risk in women aged 70–79.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 4)

items.question: Which of the following was associated with an increased risk according to WHI findings?
items.explanation: HT use was associated with an increased risk of urinary incontinence.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 5)

items.question: What effect did estrogen-progestin have on lung cancer mortality according to WHI?
items.explanation: Estrogen-progestin was associated with increased rates of lung cancer mortality.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 5)

items.question: What trend was observed in all-cause mortality for younger women using estrogen alone?
items.explanation: There was a trend toward reduced all-cause mortality in younger women using estrogen alone.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 5)

items.question: What was the relative risk of breast cancer after discontinuation of estrogen-progestin therapy?
items.explanation: An elevated risk of breast cancer persisted with an RR of 1.28 after discontinuation.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 5)

items.question: Which of the following is a contraindication for postmenopausal hormone therapy?
items.explanation: Unexplained vaginal bleeding is a contraindication for postmenopausal hormone therapy.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Postmenopausal HT (part 1) | Postmenopausal HT (part 2)

items.question: For which group of women is estrogen alone recommended?
options: Women who have had a hysterectomy
items.explanation: Estrogen alone is recommended for women who have had a hysterectomy.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Postmenopausal HT (part 1) | Postmenopausal HT (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a relative contraindication to systemic hormone therapy?
items.explanation: Unexplained vaginal bleeding is an absolute contraindication, not a relative one.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Postmenopausal HT (part 1) | Postmenopausal HT (part 2)

items.question: What should be considered when prescribing hormone therapy for primary prevention of heart disease?
options: HT is expected to prevent heart disease
HT may increase the risk of stroke and coronary artery disease
HT has no impact on cardiovascular health
items.explanation: HT should not be viewed as preventing heart disease and may increase the risk of stroke and coronary artery disease.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Postmenopausal HT (part 1) | Postmenopausal HT (part 2)

items.subtopic: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS
items.statement: FSH and estradiol levels are reliable diagnostic indicators of perimenopause in menstruating women.
items.explanation: FSH and estradiol levels have extreme intraindividual variability, making them imperfect for diagnosing perimenopause.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS

items.subtopic: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS
items.statement: Levels of FSH >25 IU/L in a random blood sample are characteristic of the late menopause transition.
items.explanation: FSH levels >25 IU/L in a random blood sample indicate the late menopause transition.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS

items.statement: Progestin-only formulations regularize menstrual cycles and reduce the number of bleeding days.
items.explanation: The passage states that progestins neither regularize cycles nor reduce the number of bleeding days.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · SYMPTOMS | PERIMENOPAUSAL THERAPY

items.statement: Nonhormonal strategies for reducing menstrual flow include nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents.
items.explanation: The passage mentions the use of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents like mefenamic acid as a nonhormonal strategy.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · SYMPTOMS | PERIMENOPAUSAL THERAPY

items.subtopic: TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT
items.statement: Observational studies have consistently confirmed that hormone therapy prevents cardiovascular disease.
items.explanation: Randomized trials, which eliminate confounding factors present in observational studies, have not consistently confirmed the benefits of HT for preventing cardiovascular disease.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT

items.subtopic: TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT
items.statement: The Women's Health Initiative (WHI) trial was stopped early due to an unfavorable benefit-risk ratio.
items.explanation: The WHI trial was stopped early because of an overall unfavorable benefit-risk ratio in the estrogen-progestin arm and an excess risk of stroke.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT

items.subtopic: BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.
items.statement: The WHI was designed to assess the effect of hormone therapy on menopausal symptoms.
items.explanation: The WHI was not designed to assess the effect of hormone therapy on menopausal symptoms.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.

items.subtopic: BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.
items.statement: Oral ospemifene is an option for treating genitourinary syndrome of menopause.
items.explanation: Oral ospemifene is listed as an additional option for treating genitourinary syndrome of menopause.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.

items.statement: Transdermal estrogen increases the risk of venous thromboembolism compared to oral estrogen.
items.explanation: Transdermal estrogen appears to be a safer alternative with respect to thrombotic risk.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 1)

items.statement: Use of progestogen eliminates the increased risk of endometrial cancer associated with unopposed estrogen.
items.explanation: Progestogen use opposes estrogen's effects on the endometrium, eliminating these risks.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 1)

items.statement: Estrogen-progestin therapy has been conclusively shown to reduce the risk of coronary heart disease.
items.explanation: Randomized trials have not confirmed cardiovascular benefits of estrogen or combined estrogen-progestin therapy.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 2)

items.statement: Transdermal HT might be a safer alternative to oral HT for reducing the risk of gallbladder disease.
items.explanation: Transdermal HT is suggested as potentially safer, but further research is needed.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 2)

items.statement: In the WHI trial, estrogen-only therapy showed an overall effect on CHD during the study.
items.explanation: No overall effect on CHD was observed with estrogen-only therapy in any specific year of follow-up.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 3)

items.statement: The KEEPS trial found that neither oral conjugated estrogens nor transdermal estradiol affected carotid atherosclerotic progression.
items.explanation: Neither form of estrogen affected carotid atherosclerotic progression, possibly due to the low prevalence of this endpoint.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 3)

items.statement: Estrogen-progestin therapy was associated with a significant reduction in colorectal cancer risk over a 5.6-year period.
items.explanation: The WHI trial found a significant 38% reduction in colorectal cancer risk with estrogen-progestin therapy.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 4)

items.statement: Postmenopausal HT has consistently shown to reduce the incidence of dementia across all age groups.
items.explanation: Subsequent randomized trials, including the WHI, have indicated a potential adverse effect of HT on the incidence of dementia in women ≥65 years.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 4)

items.statement: Estrogen-progestin therapy was associated with a significant reduction in endometrial cancer risk during the postintervention period.
items.explanation: A significant reduction in endometrial cancer risk emerged during the postintervention period.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 5)

items.statement: Estrogen-progestin therapy was associated with a favorable benefit-risk profile for all age groups.
items.explanation: Estrogen-progestin had an unfavorable benefit-risk profile overall, and it did not vary by age group.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 5)

items.statement: Transdermal estrogen is an option for women with elevated triglyceride levels.
items.explanation: Transdermal estrogen may be used because it has less adverse effects on triglyceride levels.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Postmenopausal HT (part 1) | Postmenopausal HT (part 2)

items.statement: Long-term use of hormone therapy is recommended for all postmenopausal women.
items.explanation: Long-term use is more problematic due to increased risk factors like breast cancer.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Postmenopausal HT (part 1) | Postmenopausal HT (part 2)

items.subtopic: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS
items.assertion: The propensity for anovulatory cycles during perimenopause can lead to a hyperestrogenic, hypoprogestogenic environment.
items.reason: This hormonal imbalance may account for the increased incidence of endometrial hyperplasia or carcinoma, uterine polyps, and leiomyoma in perimenopausal women.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy | ☐ PHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSTIC TESTS

items.subtopic: TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT
items.assertion: Prevention of cardiovascular disease is no longer considered a benefit of hormone therapy in postmenopausal women.
items.reason: Randomized clinical trials have not provided evidence for the benefits of hormone therapy in preventing cardiovascular disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the lack of evidence from randomized trials is the correct explanation for why prevention of cardiovascular disease is no longer considered a benefit.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · TRANSITION TO MENOPAUSE | MENOPAUSE AND POSTMENOPAUSAL HT

items.subtopic: BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.
items.assertion: Estrogen therapy is highly effective for controlling vasomotor and genitourinary symptoms of menopause.
items.reason: Alternative approaches are less effective than hormone therapy in alleviating these symptoms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why estrogen therapy is highly effective.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · BENEFITS AND RISKS OF POSTMENOPAUSAL HT See Table 407-1.

items.assertion: Estrogen therapy is associated with a reduction in vertebral fractures.
items.reason: Estrogen reduces bone turnover and resorption rates, slowing aging-related bone loss.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 1)

items.assertion: Raloxifene increases the risk of endometrial cancer.
items.reason: Raloxifene is a selective estrogen receptor modulator that does not have adverse effects on the endometrium.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; raloxifene does not increase the risk of endometrial cancer.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 1)

items.assertion: Estrogen therapy has been shown to lower LDL cholesterol levels.
items.reason: Exogenous estrogen raises HDL cholesterol levels by 10–15%.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 2)

items.reason: It promotes coagulation via factor VII and prothrombin fragments elevation.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 2)

items.assertion: The timing hypothesis suggests that starting HT closer to menopause may reduce CHD risk.
items.reason: Observational studies show lower CHD risks for women who start HT within 3–4 years of menopause compared to those ≥10 years after.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 4)

items.assertion: Estrogen-progestin therapy reduces CHD risk in women aged 70–79.
items.reason: The WHI trial showed a twofold increase in CHD risk with estrogen-progestin therapy among this age group.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 4)

items.assertion: Estrogen-only therapy was associated with a neutral benefit-risk profile in the WHI cohort as a whole.
items.reason: There was a significant trend toward a more favorable benefit-risk profile among younger women and less favorable among older women.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 5)

items.assertion: Estrogen-progestin therapy was associated with a reduction in hip fracture risk that persisted after discontinuation.
items.reason: The relative risk for hip fractures remained at 0.81 postintervention.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · OSTEOPOROSIS (See also Chap. 423) (part 5)

items.assertion: Systemic hormone therapy should be reassessed at least once every 6–12 months.
items.reason: This is to ensure the patient's continued preference for HT and any changes in health status.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Postmenopausal HT (part 1) | Postmenopausal HT (part 2)

items.assertion: Estrogen plus progestogen is recommended for women with a uterus.
items.reason: This combination reduces the risk of endometrial cancer in women with an intact uterus.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy · Postmenopausal HT (part 1) | Postmenopausal HT (part 2)
